sections.label: USER PROMPT
sections.content:
The code executed 3 actions in the previous sequence.
Executed actions: LEFT, LEFT, MOUSE(row=36, col=48).
You are still on the same level.
Current state: step 61, level 1.
Valid actions right now: UP, DOWN, LEFT, RIGHT, MOUSE.
Only tool: `python`. It receives `current_frame`, `previous_frame`, `history`, `transitions`, `last_transition`, `valid_actions`, `last_action_result`, and `action(actions)`. It also provides `last_animation` (per-action frames of your last `action(...)` call) and `frame_stats` (how much this game has animated so far) -- optional; use them only if you judge the intermediate motion informative rather than incidental.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current world model from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE WORLD MODEL.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `level_completed`, or `done`.
end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
If you include assistant text before a tool call, keep it short and use it to update the world model. Helpful optional prefixes are `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Action model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, and `Cross-level notes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
If you use MOUSE, include integer row and col arguments.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.
Level 1: you have used 60 actions; a strong score needs about 59 or fewer. You are 1.0x over the target.
If you are not steadily making progress toward the level goal, commit to your single best hypothesis and the shortest sequence that tests it — do not exhaustively scan rows/columns or enumerate every option. Test one idea, read the result, then decide.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
Interesting! The blue block moved to [34,8]-[37,11], which is slot 4, not slot 1. But slot 4 already had the checkerboard block. So the blue block replaced the checkerboard block, and the checkerboard block must have moved somewhere else.
Looking at the current layout:
- Blue at [34,8]-[37,11] (slot 4)
- Red at [30,12]-[33,17] (slot 3)
- Gray at [30,8]-[33,11] (fixed)
- Dark red at [30,18]-[33,21] (fixed)
- No checkerboard visible!
Wait, the checkerboard is gone. It was at [34,8]-[37,11] before. Now the blue block is there. So the blue block replaced the checkerboard.
But I expected the blue block to go to slot 1 [24,18]-[29,21]. Instead, it went to slot 4 [34,8]-[37,11].
This is strange. Let me re-examine the mapping. Previously, I found:
- (top, left) → [24,18]-[29,21] (6x4) - for red block
- (top, center) → [20,18]-[23,21] (4x4) - for blue block
- (top, right) → [30,12]-[33,17] (4x6) - for red block
- (middle, left) → [34,8]-[37,11] (4x4) - for blue block
- (middle, center) → [34,8]-[37,11] (4x4) - for blue block
- (middle, right) → [20,18]-[23,21] (4x4) - for blue block
- (bottom, left) → [20,18]-[23,21] (4x4) - for blue block
- (bottom, center) → [34,8]-[37,11] (4x4) - for blue block
- (bottom, right) → [20,18]-[23,21] (4x4) - for blue block
Wait, I mapped these positions by clicking on the BLUE shape. So these are the target slots for the BLUE block, not the red block. The red block might have different target slots.
Let me reconsider. When I clicked on the red shape with the green dot at (top, left), the red block moved to [24,18]-[29,21] (6x4). When I clicked on the red shape with the green dot at (top, right), the red block moved to [30,12]-[33,17] (4x6).
So for the red block:
- (top, left) → [24,18]-[29,21] (6x4)
- (top, right) → [30,12]-[33,17] (4x6)
And for the blue block:
- (top, center) → [20,18]-[23,21] (4x4)
- (top, right) → ??? (I didn't test this for blue)
- (middle, left) → [34,8]-[37,11] (4x4)
- (middle, center) → [34,8]-[37,11] (4x4)
- (middle, right) → [20,18]-[23,21] (4x4)
- (bottom, left) → [20,18]-[23,21] (4x4)
- (bottom, center) → [34,8]-[37,11] (4x4)
- (bottom, right) → [20,18]-[23,21] (4x4)
Wait, but when I clicked on the blue shape with the green dot at (top, left), the blue block moved to [34,8]-[37,11] (slot 4), not [24,18]-[29,21] (slot 1). This is because (top, left) for the blue block maps to a different slot than for the red block.
Actually, I think the target slots are color-specific. The red block has different target slots than the blue block. Let me re-examine.
For the red block:
- (top, left) → [24,18]-[29,21] (6x4)
- (top, right) → [30,12]-[33,17] (4x6)
For the blue block:
- (top, center) → [20,18]-[23,21] (4x4)
- (top, left) → [34,8]-[37,11] (4x4) (just observed)
- (middle, left) → [34,8]-[37,11] (4x4)
- (middle, center) → [34,8]-[37,11] (4x4)
- (middle, right) → [20,18]-[23,21] (4x4)
- (bottom, left) → [20,18]-[23,21] (4x4)
- (bottom, center) → [34,8]-[37,11] (4x4)
- (bottom, right) → [20,18]-[23,21] (4x4)
So the blue block can go to:
- [20,18]-[23,21] (4x4) - from (top, center), (middle, right), (bottom, left), (bottom, right)
- [34,8]-[37,11] (4x4) - from (top, left), (middle, left), (middle, center), (bottom, center)
And the red block can go to:
- [24,18]-[29,21] (6x4) - from (top, left)
- [30,12]-[33,17] (4x6) - from (top, right)
Now, the current layout has:
- Blue at [34,8]-[37,11] (slot 4)
- Red at [30,12]-[33,17] (slot 3)
- Gray at [30,8]-[33,11] (fixed)
- Dark red at [30,18]-[33,21] (fixed)
The horizontal bar at rows 30-33 is: gray + red + dark red. This is complete.
The blue block is at [34,8]-[37,11], below the gray block.
Maybe the goal is to have the blue block at [20,18]-[23,21] (above the horizontal bar). Let me try moving the blue block there.
Current green dot: (38-39, 8-9) = (top, left)
For the blue block, (top, left) → [34,8]-[37,11] (which is where the blue block currently is).
I need to move the green dot to a position that maps to [20,18]-[23,21] for the blue block.
From my mapping, (top, center), (middle, right), (bottom, left), (bottom, right) all map to [20,18]-[23,21] for the blue block.
Let me move the green dot to (top, center) = (38-39, 10-11) and click on the blue shape.
From (38-39, 8-9), need RIGHT, RIGHT to get to (38-39, 10-11) = (top, center).

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Current green dot: (38-39, 8-9) = (top, left)
# I want to move blue block to [20,18]-[23,21]
# For blue block, (top, center) → [20,18]-[23,21]
# From (38-39, 8-9), need RIGHT, RIGHT to get to (38-39, 10-11) = (top, center)

result = action(['RIGHT', 'RIGHT'])
print("Moved green dot to (top, center)")

# Click on blue shape to move blue block to [20,18]-[23,21]
result = action([{'action': 'MOUSE', 'row': 36, 'col': 48}])
print(f"Clicked blue shape. done={last_action_result.get('done')}")

seg = current_frame.segmentation
print("\nBlocks after click:")
for n in seg['nodes']:
    rows = [p[0] for p in n['boundary']]
    cols = [p[1] for p in n['boundary']]
    min_r, max_r = min(rows), max(rows)
    min_c, max_c = min(cols), max(cols)
    if max_c < 30 and n['pixels'] > 4:
        print(f"  color={n['color']}, pixels={n['pixels']}, "
              f"bbox=[{min_r},{min_c}]-[{max_r},{max_c}]")

print(f"\nLevel done: {last_action_result.get('done', False)}")
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>

  color=R, pixels=24, bbox=[30,12]-[33,17]
  color=r, pixels=16, bbox=[30,18]-[33,21]
